<p>Platform of Computing Resources Application and Submission: <a href="https://flagos.io/RaceDetail?id=296fmsd8&lang=en" target="_blank" aria-label="Task 3: LLM Automatic Data Annotation (opens in a new tab)" rel="noreferrer nofollow">Task 3: LLM Automatic Data Annotation</a></p>
<p>OpenSeek repository: <a href="https://github.com/FlagAI-Open/OpenSeek/tree/main/openseek/competition/LongContext-ICL-Annotation" target="_blank" aria-label="OpenSeek: LongContext-ICL-Annotation (opens in a new tab)" rel="noreferrer nofollow">OpenSeek: LongContext-ICL-Annotation</a></p>
<hr>
<p>In the era of large language models (LLMs), high-quality annotated data has become a critical driving force behind rapid advances in model capability. Whether for pre-training, fine-tuning, alignment, or domain adaptation, the upper bound of model performance is largely determined by the scale, diversity, and annotation quality of the training data. However, traditional human annotation is costly, time-consuming, and difficult to scale to complex tasks involving long-range semantics, which severely limits the practical deployment of LLMs in specialized domains and long-text scenarios.</p>
<p>In-context learning (ICL), as a few-shot learning paradigm that requires no parameter updates, has demonstrated remarkable flexibility and plug-and-play usability. By incorporating input–output examples into prompts, LLMs can infer task intent and generalize to unseen samples, achieving performance comparable to or even exceeding that of fine-tuned models in many NLP tasks, while significantly reducing reliance on large-scale annotated data.</p>
<p>Nevertheless, existing ICL research has predominantly focused on short or medium-length contexts. In ultra-long context settings that span tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands of tokens, the effectiveness, robustness, and scalability of ICL remain insufficiently explored. From one perspective, large collections of example texts encode rich task- and domain-specific information, which makes it difficult to efficiently select, order, and organize demonstrations and instructions without introducing interference, ambiguity, or attention dilution. From another perspective, ultra-long contexts often contain abundant structured and unstructured information, thereby imposing substantially higher requirements on a model’s memory capacity, reasoning ability, and key information extraction. As a result, simple single-step in-context inference is no longer adequate. Instead, effective solutions require the integration of multi-step reasoning and analytical strategies to support collaborative annotation frameworks that exhibit perception, planning, and execution capabilities.</p>
<p>This track focuses on complex, real-world ultra-long text scenarios and aims to explore the capabilities and practical approaches of large language models (LLMs) for automated data annotation. Participants are encouraged to develop long-context in-context learning (ICL) frameworks with effective example selection, context organization, and multi-step reasoning, in order to improve annotation accuracy, robustness, and scalability toward human-level performance. The competition seeks to bring together ideas from both academia and industry, promoting reproducible and open solutions that support the development of high-quality, cost-effective, and scalable intelligent data systems.</p></div></div></div><div class="sc-doveL fzozFY"><div class="sc-kuXGAk dHanwc"><div class="sc-bioDha eqSTIQ sc-dsJwGW eXRtvW"><div class="sc-facuLB hmGruf"><div <li>Evaluation results will be summarized and ranked on a standardized leaderboard using a unified evaluation pipeline.</li>

<p>The organizing committee will conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the submitted application materials and make allocation decisions based on the following criteria:</p>

<h2>Competition Stage</h2>
<p>March 24, 2026 – May 20, 2026 (GMT+8)</p>
<p>During this stage, the official evaluation leaderboard will be opened. Participating teams are required to conduct model inference in accordance with the competition requirements and submit their prediction results to the competition platform. In addition, teams must submit the technical report and complete experimental code corresponding to their final competition solution to the platform before the end of this stage.</p>
<ul>
<li>Leaderboard Submission Rules:<ul>
<li>Each team may submit up to five entries per day. The system will automatically record all submissions and retain the best-performing result for leaderboard ranking.</li>
<li>The leaderboard will display each team’s best evaluation performance on the test set.</li>
<li>Teams may iteratively refine their solutions based on leaderboard feedback, provided that all submissions strictly comply with the competition requirements regarding model selection, computing resource usage, and development frameworks.</li></ul></li>
<li>Technical Report and Code Submission Rules:<ul>
<li>Technical reports and source code must be submitted in full accordance with the competition requirements, ensuring standardized formatting, clear structure, and full reproducibility.</li>
<li>The experimental code must include comprehensive environment configuration instructions, model loading and inference scripts, as well as the necessary documentation for usage and execution.</li>
<li>Teams that fail to submit the technical report or source code within the specified timeframe, or whose submissions are incomplete or not reproducible, will be deemed to have failed to complete the required competition procedures, and their results will not be considered in the final evaluation or ranking.</li></ul></li>
</ul>
<p>After this stage concludes, the submission portal will be closed, and no further submissions of any kind will be accepted. The leaderboard will then be officially frozen, and the frozen leaderboard scores will constitute a critical component of the final evaluation. Reviewers will rely exclusively on the technical reports and source code submitted through the competition platform as the sole basis for assessing the technical solution scores.</p>
<h2>Open-Source Submission of Technical Reports and Code</h2>
<p>May 21, 2026 – May 31, 2026 (GMT+8)</p>
<p>To promote technical exchange and ensure transparency and reproducibility, all participating teams are required to publicly release their technical reports and complete source code. All materials must be submitted under the official OpenSeek open-source project.</p>
<p>Official OpenSeek Project Repository:<br>
<a href="https://github.com/FlagAI-Open/OpenSeek" target="_blank" aria-label="https://github.com/FlagAI-Open/OpenSeek (opens in a new tab)" rel="noreferrer nofollow">https://github.com/FlagAI-Open/OpenSeek</a></p>
<p>The specific requirements are as follows:</p>
<ul>
<li><p>Participating teams are required to submit their technical report and source code to the official <strong>OpenSeek repository</strong> in the form of a Pull Request (PR), and to provide the corresponding PR link on the relevant competition page of the <a href="https://flagos.io/RaceDetail?id=296fmsd8&lang=en" target="_blank" aria-label="FlagOS platform (opens in a new tab)" rel="noreferrer nofollow">FlagOS platform</a>.</p></li>
<li><p>The technical report and source code must match the solution corresponding to the team’s final submission on the leaderboard during the competition stage.</p></li>
<li><p>All submitted materials must fully comply with the competition requirements in terms of format, completeness, and reproducibility.</p></li>
<li><p>Teams that fail to complete the open-source submission within the specified deadline, or whose submissions are incomplete or do not match their final competition results, will be considered to have not fulfilled the competition requirements and will be disqualified from the final evaluation and ranking.</p></li>
</ul>
<h2>Technical Solution Evaluation</h2>
<p>May 21, 2026 – June 4, 2026 (GMT+8)</p>
<p>The Organizing Committee will conduct a centralized review and reproducibility verification of the technical reports and reproducible code submitted by participating teams via the competition platform. The specific evaluation items and scoring criteria are detailed in the relevant competition requirements.</p>
<p>If a participating team falls under any of the following circumstances, its technical solution score will be recorded as zero and will not be included in the final ranking:</p>
<ul>
<li>Failure to submit a technical report or complete source code in accordance with the competition requirements;</li>
<li>Inconsistencies between the submitted technical report and source code, or failure of the source code to successfully reproduce the experimental results corresponding to the competition stage;</li>
<li>Failure to complete the open-source release of the technical report and source code within the specified timeframe and in the prescribed manner.</li>
</ul>
<h2>Results Announcement and Awards</h2>
<p>Early June 2026 (GMT+8)</p>
<p>Final rankings will be determined based on a weighted combination of:</p>
<ul>
<li>Prediction Performance Score: 60%</li>
<li>Technical Solution Score: 40%</li>
</ul>

<h3>2. Technical Solution Score (40 %)</h3>
<p>The organizing committee will reproduce submitted code and evaluate the technical solution in conjunction with the technical report, focusing on the depth and effectiveness of solutions to the following core challenges:</p>
<ul>
<li>Prompt and instruction design for stable, high-quality annotation in ultra-long contexts (20%)</li>
<li>How to construct information-dense and structurally coherent ultra-long contexts when the number of available annotated examples significantly exceeds the model’s context capacity (40%)</li>
<li>Efficient utilization of ultra-long contexts in multi-turn or continuous annotation settings, balancing consistency and scalability (40%)<br>
<br><br>
<br><br>
<strong>Notes:</strong></li>
<li>Solutions that cannot be successfully reproduced will be disqualified from final ranking.</li>
<li>During the reproducibility phase, the organizing committee will deploy the Qwen3-4B in a unified hardware environment and conduct reproduction experiments on the solutions submitted by the participating teams.</li>
